arguments.text:
Council members, the plaintiff has now supplied the source chain the defense correctly demanded. The preponderance answer is Yes.

PX-1 gives the governing test. The qualifying item may be official, leaked, or otherwise disclosed; the key is that by May 8 at 11:59 PM ET a note/message was publicly available and credibly reported to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication. PX-1 also cautions that market metadata alone is not independent proof. Plaintiff therefore does not ask you to decide from prices or a proposed UMA result.

PX-2 is the primary source for public release. In United States v. Tartaglione, Judge Kenneth Karas of the Southern District of New York described the requested material as an alleged “suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein,” recovered by Tartaglione and reportedly submitted by Tartaglione’s attorneys. The order states that the New York Times sought unsealing on April 30, 2026. Most importantly, the court granted the Times’ request as to the Note, found the Note subject to a public-access presumption, found no sufficient reason to keep it sealed, and rejected continued sealing based on privilege or privacy. That proves a court-related note matching the market description was ordered into public access before the May 8 deadline.

PX-3 and PX-4 prove contemporaneous public availability and credible reporting before the deadline. PX-3 was published May 6, 2026 at 7:30 PM EDT. It reports that a federal judge unsealed the purported Epstein suicide note that Wednesday, identifies Judge Karas’s ruling, credits court documents, and gives the apparent text. PX-4 was published May 6, 2026 at 9:01 PM EDT. It likewise reports that Judge Karas unsealed what appeared to be a handwritten note from Epstein and reproduces the material wording. Both publications were public more than two days before the May 8, 11:59 PM ET deadline.

The note also satisfies the “suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication” element by a preponderance. PX-2 shows the judicially unsealed item was described to the court as a suicide note purportedly authored by Epstein. PX-3 and PX-4 report language consistent with a farewell or self-harm communication: “It is a treat to be able to choose one’s time to say goodbye,” followed by “NO FUN” and “NOT WORTH IT” phrasing. The market rule does not require a formal title, signature, or official suicide-investigation finding; it requires credible reporting of authorship and intent. The admitted evidence clears that threshold.

The defense’s strongest point is provenance, and the plaintiff accepts it as a weight issue. PX-4 states the document was not dated or signed and had not been verified. PX-3 and PX-4 trace the chain through Nicholas Tartaglione, a former cellmate serving a life sentence, who claimed to have found the note in a book or graphic novel. Those facts prevent overclaiming. They mean the record does not prove with certainty that Epstein wrote it, and the council should not treat it as officially authenticated.

But the proposition was deliberately phrased around credible reporting, not conclusive authentication. The admitted sources are not rumor alone: a federal court order identifies the Note, grants unsealing, and finds public access appropriate; contemporaneous public reports before the deadline identify it as the purported Epstein suicide note, report the court provenance, reproduce its apparent text, and disclose the verification caveats. That is exactly the kind of credible public reporting the rule contemplates.

So the remaining uncertainty does not defeat the claim. It narrows it: by May 8, 2026, the public had access to a court-unsealed note credibly reported as a purported Epstein suicide/farewell note, though not officially verified. On a preponderance standard, that is enough. The council should find the proposition true.

Council members, the plaintiff has improved the record on one element but still asks the council to take a step the evidence does not justify. The record now supports that a court-related document was made public and that several outlets reported on it before the May 8 deadline. The harder elements are authorship and intent: whether this was credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication. On this record, those elements remain too uncertain for preponderance.

Start with PX-2. The SDNY order is an access ruling, not an authentication ruling. Judge Karas granted public access to a document the New York Times described as an alleged “suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein,” recovered by Tartaglione and reportedly submitted by Tartaglione’s attorneys. That matters, but the court’s public-access analysis did not find that Epstein wrote it, did not find that it was a suicide note, and did not resolve chain of custody. It found only that the Note was a judicial document with a presumption of access and no sufficient sealing justification.

PX-3 and PX-4 have the same limitation. FOX Local says the note “may have been written” by Epstein and calls it “purported.” People says the document was not dated, not signed, and “has not been verified.” People also traces the source to Nicholas Tartaglione’s allegation that he found it inside a graphic novel after Epstein was found injured in July 2019. Those are not small footnotes. The proposition does not ask whether a paper was public; it asks whether a qualifying note was credibly reported as Epstein-written and suicide/final-message in character.

DX-1 adds the strongest limiting context. The PBS/AP report confirms the public-release event, but states plainly that it is unclear who wrote the note Tartaglione claimed to have found. It further reports that the note was not mentioned in the lengthy government reports examining Epstein’s death and did not surface in the Justice Department’s recent release of Epstein files. And it distinguishes the handwritten note officials found in Epstein’s cell at the time of his death: officials said that separate note did not appear to be a suicide note, but rather a list of grievances about jail conditions. That makes the source-chain problem sharper, not weaker.

The plaintiff emphasizes the phrase “It is a treat to be able to choose one’s time to say goodbye” and the “NO FUN / NOT WORTH IT” ending. Those words can support a suspicion of suicidal meaning. But they are embedded in a short, hard-to-decipher, unsigned, undated, unverified paper whose provenance begins with a convicted murderer and former cellmate. The wording also includes complaints about investigations and old charges. The council can see why reporters discussed it as a possible suicide note. But the market rule requires credible reporting that it was written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent. Hedges like alleged, purported, may have been written, appears, and not verified are doing real work here.

Nor does “credible reporting” mean every caveated report counts as proof. Credible outlets can credibly report uncertainty. That is what happened here. They reported the release of a disputed item, disclosed Tartaglione’s claim, repeated the apparent text, and warned that authorship was unclear or unverified. Treating that as a resolved qualifying note would convert reporting about an allegation into proof of the allegation.

The defendant therefore asks for a careful finding: plaintiff has shown public availability of a court-unsealed document before the deadline, but has not shown by a preponderance that the document was credibly established as Epstein-written and intended as a suicide note or final message. The admitted evidence proves a public controversy about a purported note. It does not prove the qualifying market condition. The proposition should be found false.

case.caption: Claimant v. Respondent

closings.text:
Council members, this case is decided by the words “credibly reported,” not by a demand for official authentication. The plaintiff does not ask you to find that the note has been proven genuine beyond dispute. The record does not show that. The record shows something narrower, and sufficient: before May 8, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET, a note was publicly available and credibly reported as a purported Epstein suicide/farewell note.

Start with the deadline and public availability. PX-2 is the official SDNY order. On May 6, 2026, Judge Karas granted the Times’ request to unseal the Note, a document described in that order as an alleged “suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein,” recovered by Tartaglione and reportedly submitted by his attorneys. PX-3 was published May 6 at 7:30 PM EDT; PX-4 was published May 6 at 9:01 PM EDT; DX-1/PX-R1 was published May 6 at 8:01 PM EDT and updated May 7. All are before the May 8 deadline. The public-availability element is not seriously in doubt.

Next, credible reporting. This is not a stray social-media claim or market rumor. The source chain in the record includes an official federal court order, reporting tied to court documents, AP reporting carried by PBS, and other contemporaneous public reports. The defense’s own evidence confirms that Judge Karas ordered release after the Times request, that prosecutors did not oppose, and that the note was made public Wednesday. That is credible reporting under the market rule.

The authorship and intent elements are where the defense focuses, but the same record satisfies them at the required level. PX-2 identifies the court-unsealed item as a suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein. PX-3 reports it as the purported suicide note and reproduces apparent text. PX-4 reports that Judge Karas unsealed what appears to be a handwritten note from Epstein. PX-R1’s image caption identifies the note as purported to be written by Epstein and released by the U.S. District Court. This is exactly “credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein,” even though the reporting keeps the proper caveats.

Intent is also supported by more than a label. The admitted reports preserve wording about being able to choose the time to say goodbye, followed by “Bust out cryin,” “NO FUN,” and “NOT WORTH IT.” In ordinary context, a document publicly described as a suicide note and containing farewell/self-harm language qualifies at least as a final message or equivalent communication. The rule did not require a formal salutation, signature, date, or official medical finding.

The contrary evidence is real but not decisive. The note was unsigned, undated, unverified, and passed through Tartaglione. AP/PBS says it was unclear who wrote it, and notes it was absent from government death reports and later DOJ file releases. Those facts prevent a finding that Epstein certainly wrote it. But certainty is not the standard, and authenticity is not the proposition. The proposition asks whether the public record by the deadline credibly reported a qualifying note. The caveats are part of that credible reporting; they do not erase the reporting.

The separate death-cell grievance note is likewise not disqualifying. The record distinguishes it from the Tartaglione/Curcio Note. The grievance note shows only that another handwritten document existed at Epstein’s death and was not treated as a suicide note. It does not rebut that this separate court-unsealed Note was credibly reported as a purported Epstein suicide/farewell note.

On balance, the plaintiff’s side has the better preponderance. The record contains a court-unsealed Note before the deadline, multiple credible public reports, a court-document source chain, and farewell-style text. The defense has important uncertainty, but it asks you to convert “not authenticated” into “not credibly reported.” That is not the market rule. The proposition should be found true.

Council members, the plaintiff’s best point is public availability. The defense accepts that the record now supports a May 6 public-release event and public reporting before the May 8 deadline. That is not enough to decide this proposition. The proposition requires a message or note credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication.

The difference between “a disputed paper was released” and “a qualifying Epstein-written suicide/final note was credibly reported” is the whole case.

PX-2, the SDNY order, is the strongest source for the plaintiff. But it is not an authentication finding. It is a public-access ruling. The order describes the object as an alleged “suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein” because that was the material the Times sought to unseal, recovered through Tartaglione and reportedly submitted by Tartaglione’s lawyers. Judge Karas decided access and sealing. He did not find that Epstein wrote it. He did not find that it was intended as a suicide note. He did not resolve the Tartaglione chain of custody.

The press record is equally careful. PX-3 says the note “may have been written” by Epstein. PX-4 says it was not dated, not signed, and had not been verified. DX-1, the AP/PBS report, states that it is unclear who wrote the note Tartaglione claimed to have found; that the note was not mentioned in the lengthy government reports examining Epstein’s death; and that it did not surface in the Justice Department’s later file release. Those are record facts, not speculation.

The plaintiff says the rule requires credible reporting, not certainty. Correct. But credible reporting of uncertainty is still uncertainty. A credible article can report that a document is alleged, purported, unverified, and of unclear authorship. That does not become proof of authorship and intent merely because the outlet is credible. The council should not take the reporting, remove every qualifier, and then call the stripped-down version a preponderance finding.

Intent has the same problem. The wording reported in the articles can be read as dark or farewell-like. It can support why the item drew attention. But the record also shows the paper was short, hard to decipher in places, unsigned, undated, unverified, and routed through a convicted murderer and former cellmate. It also shows that officials had a separate handwritten note in Epstein’s cell at the time of his death and said that note did not appear to be a suicide note. That does not disprove the Tartaglione note; it shows why the council must be careful not to infer “suicide note” from Epstein’s later death alone.

Finally, note what is missing. No party admitted the actual court-note image or PDF as a standalone artifact. No party admitted a handwriting report. No party admitted an official finding that Epstein authored this paper or intended it as a final message. Plaintiff’s own technical ledger acknowledges the gap: the actual court-note image/PDF was not separately admitted, and the record relies on reports describing and reproducing material text and caveats.

So the record proves a public allegation, and it proves that allegation was reported by credible outlets before the deadline. It does not prove, by a preponderance, the qualifying condition as written. The careful qualifiers are not decoration; they are the evidence. Because authorship and intent remain unresolved at the point the rule requires them to be credibly established, the proposition should be found false.

council_votes.rationale: While the record shows public availability of a disputed document before the deadline, the credible reporting contains substantial uncertainty about authorship and intent. The PBS/AP report explicitly states 'It is unclear who wrote the note' and notes its absence from official investigations, preventing a preponderance finding that it was credibly reported as Epstein-written and intended as a suicide note.

The record shows a federal court order on May 6, 2026, granting public access to a document described as a purported suicide note by Jeffrey Epstein, along with contemporaneous credible reports before the May 8 deadline that credibly reported the note's public availability and content.

The record shows a court order on May 6, 2026, granting public access to a document described as a purported suicide note by Jeffrey Epstein, along with contemporaneous credible reporting before the May 8 deadline that reproduced the text and acknowledged the limitations, satisfying the preponderance standard.

Admitted evidence shows a court order unsealing a document described as a suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein and multiple credible outlets reported it before the May 8 deadline as a purported Epstein suicide note with farewell language. While reports note uncertainty, they credibly report the note as written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note. On a preponderance basis the proposition is satisfied.

The federal court order released a note described as a suicide note authored by Epstein, and credible reports before the May 8 deadline reproduced its text and characterized it as a suicide/farewell note, meeting the preponderance standard.

offered_evidence.label: PX-1 Market rule and case-packet framing
PX-2 SDNY order granting unsealing of the Note
PX-3 FOX Local contemporaneous report and note text
PX-4 People contemporaneous report with caveats
DX-1 PBS/AP report on authorship uncertainty, government-report omission, and death-cell note distinction
DX-2 SDNY order as access ruling, not authentication finding
DX-3 People report with unsigned, undated, unverified caveats
PX-R1 Defendant AP/PBS report confirming public release and caveats
DX-S1 AP/PBS caveats on authorship, government-report omission, and separate death-cell note
DX-S2 People caveats: unsigned, undated, unverified

openings.text:
Council members, the proposition is narrow. It does not ask whether Jeffrey Epstein’s death was investigated correctly, whether Polymarket or UMA resolved a market correctly, or whether an official agency has conclusively authenticated a document. It asks whether, by May 8, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET, a message or note credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication had been made publicly available.

The starting record already frames the dispute. The case-packet evidence, ev_90fa205907f7_market-page, preserves the relevant market rule: a qualifying note may be official, leaked, or otherwise disclosed, and the resolution source is a consensus of credible reporting. The packet identifies the specific May 8 market and deadline. It also records that, in the Polymarket event metadata captured after the fact, the public controversy centered on a reported May 7, 2026 unsealing by a U.S. District Judge in New York of a purported suicide note attributed to Epstein. Just as important, the packet warns that this platform metadata is not independent proof of the proposition and that authenticity and intent doubts exist.

That is the real dispute. The plaintiff’s case should not rest on market prices, proposed outcomes, or a platform’s summary. Those facts help identify the question, but they do not themselves establish the underlying event. The decisive proof will be the source chain: the court order or docket material showing public release, the released note or a faithful reproduction/extraction of it, and credible reporting around the release that explains how the note was attributed, why it was treated as a suicide note or final communication, and what limitations remain.

On the burden, the plaintiff need only prove the proposition by a preponderance. The rule itself requires credible reporting, not official certainty. A document can qualify even if later debate remains about handwriting, motive, or chain of custody, so long as the public record by the deadline credibly reported it as a note written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication. Conversely, if the later evidence shows only rumor, market chatter, an unavailable sealed filing, or a document not plausibly tied to Epstein or not plausibly a final/suicide communication, the plaintiff should lose.

The record also tells us what weaknesses must be confronted rather than hidden. The packet flags doubts about authenticity and intent. The expected evidence path involves a note reportedly recovered through Epstein’s former cellmate and a criminal docket, not a clean release from Epstein’s estate or from an official investigation into his death. Those provenance facts affect weight. They may limit what can be said about whether Epstein actually wrote the note. But they do not automatically defeat the proposition, because the proposition is about public availability and credible reporting by the deadline, not absolute authentication beyond dispute.

The plaintiff therefore expects to show, in the evidence phase, that before the May 8 deadline the public had access to a released court-related note and that credible outlets and court materials described it as a purported Epstein suicide note or equivalent farewell communication. The plaintiff also expects to submit the adverse context: any uncertainty over authorship, whether the note was unsigned or undated, and the limits of the chain of custody. If that evidence comes in as expected, the fair preponderance conclusion will be that the qualifying public availability threshold was met by May 8, even while the council discounts the evidence for unresolved authenticity questions.

For opening, the request is simple: decide this case on the underlying dated public release and credible reporting, not on the market’s economics. The existing packet points to the precise factual issue and the source materials that should decide it. The plaintiff will carry the burden by admitting those source materials when the rules permit evidence submission.

Council members, the defendant’s position is straightforward: the plaintiff may ultimately find leads worth admitting, but the present record does not yet prove the proposition by a preponderance. It proves that a prediction market existed, that its rules framed a May 8 deadline, and that the market was disputed. It does not prove that a qualifying Epstein-written suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication was publicly available by 11:59 PM ET on May 8, 2026.

The single case-packet exhibit, ev_90fa205907f7_market-page, is useful but limited. It preserves Polymarket’s rule text and the May 8 market identifiers. It also records that later Polymarket metadata described a reported New York federal-court unsealing of a purported Epstein suicide note. But the packet itself warns that this platform context is not independent proof of the proposition and that authenticity and intent doubts exist. That warning should govern this case from the start.

The proposition has several links, and the plaintiff must carry each one. First, there must be a message or note actually made publicly available by the deadline. Second, it must be credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein. Third, it must be credibly reported as intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication. Fourth, the public availability must be shown with a reliable dated source, not inferred from a market page, a proposed resolution, trading prices, or social-media repetition.

The decisive evidence, if it exists, should be concrete: the court docket or order, the actual unsealed document or faithful reproduction, and contemporaneous credible reporting that fixes the date, describes the source chain, and explains the basis for attributing authorship and intent to Epstein. The current record contains none of those underlying materials. It contains only a market packet summarizing that such a controversy was discussed.

The defense does not need to show at opening that no such document exists. The burden is on the plaintiff. And even if a document is later admitted, the council should examine its weight carefully. A document called a “purported suicide note” is not automatically a qualifying note under this market rule. Provenance through a third party, an unsigned or undated document, sealed or partially sealed surrounding records, lack of official authentication, or a press label that outruns the document’s actual contents would all matter. Those facts may not be fatal individually, but together they can prevent the plaintiff from crossing the preponderance line on authorship and intent.

So the defense asks the council to separate three things: market metadata, public reporting, and proof of the qualifying event. The first is already in the packet. The second may become relevant only when admitted in full enough to evaluate credibility and timing. The third is the plaintiff’s burden. Until the primary source chain is in the record and tested, this case should not be decided on the assumption that market chatter or a platform summary equals a publicly available Epstein suicide note.

case.proposition: By May 8, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, a message or note credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication had been made publicly available.

rebuttals.text:
Council members, the defense’s argument depends on quietly changing the proposition. It argues as though plaintiff must prove the note was officially authenticated as Epstein’s genuine suicide note. But PX-1 asks whether a message or note was “credibly reported” to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent. That wording accepts that the qualifying public source may be reporting, and that the item may be publicly disclosed by any means.

The defendant is right about one thing: PX-2 is an access ruling, not a handwriting finding. But it is still powerful primary evidence because it identifies the exact object the Times sought to unseal: an alleged “suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein,” recovered by Tartaglione and submitted in Tartaglione’s case. The court then granted public access to that Note before the May 8 deadline. Plaintiff never claimed Judge Karas authenticated the handwriting. The point is that the public-access event involved the very category of document the rule describes.

The defense’s own AP/PBS evidence, PX-R1, strengthens rather than defeats the plaintiff’s showing. It states that the note was made public Wednesday; that Judge Karas ordered its release after the Times asked to unseal it; that prosecutors did not oppose; and that the image caption identified it as a note purported to be written by Epstein and released by the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York. It also reports the same farewell text: choosing the time to say goodbye, “Bust out cryin,” “NO FUN,” and “NOT WORTH IT.” That is not just market chatter. It is AP reporting, carried by PBS, describing a court-released document before the deadline.

The defense emphasizes “unclear who wrote it,” “not verified,” unsigned, undated, and not in government death reports. Those are real caveats, but they are caveats to certainty, not disqualifiers under this rule. Credible reporting often reports an allegation with careful language: “purported,” “alleged,” “may have been written.” The market rule did not say “confirmed by DOJ,” “signed,” “authenticated,” or “found in an official death investigation.” It said credibly reported to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note/final message/equivalent. Multiple admitted sources did exactly that while disclosing uncertainty.

Nor does the separate death-cell grievance note defeat this note. PX-R1 distinguishes that other document: officials found a separate handwritten note at Epstein’s death that appeared to concern jail grievances. That does not make the Tartaglione/Curcio Note nonqualifying. It simply prevents confusion between two different papers. The relevant court-released Note is the one described in PX-2, PX-3, PX-4, and PX-R1.

The preponderance balance is therefore straightforward. On one side: an official SDNY order granting access to a document described as a purported Epstein suicide note, contemporaneous May 6 reporting by multiple outlets, AP/PBS confirmation, court-document image attribution, and farewell-style wording. On the other side: unresolved authorship and source-chain doubts. Those doubts reduce weight, but they do not erase the credible reporting. The proposition should be found true.

submitted_evidence.relevance: Primary court source establishing that the New York Times sought to unseal a note described as a suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein; that the court granted unsealing of the Note; and that the Note was subject to public access.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Text extraction from official Southern District of New York PDF order, United States v. Nicholas Tartaglione, Case No. 16-CR-832 (KMK), Order by Judge Kenneth M. Karas dated May 6, 2026.
submitted_evidence.title: SDNY May 6, 2026 order granting unsealing of Epstein Note in United States v. Tartaglione

submitted_evidence.relevance: Contemporaneous public report before the May 8 deadline stating a federal judge unsealed the purported Epstein suicide note and reproducing the note's apparent text.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Captured relevant text from FOX Local/FOX 13 Tampa Bay article by Catherine Stoddard, published May 6, 2026 7:30 PM EDT.
submitted_evidence.title: FOX Local May 6, 2026 report: Epstein purported suicide note unsealed and full text reported

submitted_evidence.relevance: Contemporaneous public report before the May 8 deadline corroborating unsealing, note wording, and key adverse caveats that the document was not dated, signed, or verified and came through Tartaglione.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Captured relevant text from People article by Charmaine Patterson, published May 6, 2026 9:01 PM EDT.
submitted_evidence.title: People May 6, 2026 report: verification caveats and note provenance

submitted_evidence.relevance: Adverse/limiting contemporaneous reporting on the alleged note's provenance, public release, unclear authorship, absence from government death reports and DOJ file releases, and separate official death-cell note that officials said did not appear to be a suicide note.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Relevant captured text from PBS NewsHour article by Associated Press reporters Michael R. Sisak and Larry Neumeister, published May 6, 2026 8:01 PM EDT and updated May 7, 2026 5:36 PM EDT.
submitted_evidence.title: PBS/AP May 6-7, 2026 report: authorship uncertainty and DOJ-report omission

surrebuttals.text:
Council members, the plaintiff’s rebuttal usefully narrows the issue. The defense is not asking for official authentication, a DOJ confirmation, or a signed note. The market rule does not require that. But the rule does require more than credible reporting that a disputed paper was released. It requires that the paper be credibly reported to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication.

That distinction matters because the admitted reporting is careful reporting of uncertainty. PX-2 is an access order. Plaintiff concedes it is not a handwriting finding. It identifies the object as an alleged note purportedly authored by Epstein because that is how the Times request and Tartaglione source chain framed it. The court made the document public; it did not find that Epstein wrote it or that it was intended as a suicide note.

The same is true of the press record. PX-3 says the note “may have been written” by Epstein. PX-4 says the document is not dated, not signed, and has not been verified. DX-1/PX-R1 says it is unclear who wrote the note Tartaglione claimed to have found, that the note was absent from the government reports examining Epstein’s death, and that it did not surface in the Justice Department’s later Epstein-file release. Those are not merely lawyerly caveats to certainty; they are the central evidentiary facts about authorship.

Plaintiff is right that the separate death-cell grievance note does not itself disprove the Tartaglione note. The defense offered that fact for a narrower reason: it shows why the council should not slide from “Epstein’s death was a suicide” to “this paper was Epstein’s suicide note.” Officials had a different handwritten note at the time of death and said that one did not appear to be a suicide note. The document at issue here comes from an unrelated Tartaglione case and a disputed third-party chain.

So the final question is not whether credible outlets covered the release. They did. It is whether their coverage credibly resolves the required facts. It does not. The reporting says: alleged, purported, may have been written, appears, unclear who wrote it, not dated, not signed, not verified. A preponderance finding cannot be built by stripping those qualifiers away. The record proves public availability of a controversial alleged note before May 8. It does not prove the qualifying condition as written. The proposition should be found false.

technical_reports.summary: Record was scanned before filing. The original packet was the only preexisting evidence. Plaintiff searched targeted terms for Epstein, Tartaglione, Judge Karas, SDNY, May 6-7 2026, and court documents. Plaintiff found the official SDNY order PDF, downloaded it locally, recorded size 128,992 bytes and SHA-256 7225441cb802084e2b9485ebc2c6d246fbfa08e109fe650cb14f445dc4745ca0. Local pdftotext was unavailable, so plaintiff submitted a relevant text extraction from the official PDF rather than a truncated PDF. Plaintiff also captured contemporaneous FOX Local and People reports because they supply public-release timing, note wording, and adverse caveats. Remaining gap: the actual court-note image/PDF was not separately admitted; the admitted reports describe and reproduce the material text and caveats.
technical_reports.title: Merits source-chain ledger

technical_reports.summary: Record was scanned before filing. Plaintiff's new evidence was read directly: SDNY order extraction, FOX Local report, and People report. Defense searched DOJ/Tartaglione/Epstein note terms and checked PBS/AP and Al Jazeera/Reuters reporting. Defense submitted PBS/AP because it preserved the most material limiting facts: unclear authorship, absence from government death reports and DOJ file releases, provenance through Tartaglione, and distinction from the separate note officials found in Epstein's death cell. Remaining gap: no party has admitted the actual note image/PDF as a standalone artifact or a handwriting/authentication record; the court order is a public-access ruling, not an authenticity determination.
technical_reports.title: Defense source-chain ledger
